feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced low-nitrogen (e.g. 10-10-10), worked in at planting; avoid high-nitrogen feeds that promote leaves over flowers. RHS option: potassium-rich tomato-type feed once flower buds form.

commonIssues: Direct-sow only after the last spring frost once soil has warmed to at least 10C (50F); seedlings and plants are frost-sensitive. Sunflowers dislike root disturbance, so direct sowing is preferred over transplanting.

Container plants dry out fast and need watering more frequently than in-ground plantings, potentially daily in hot weather; check the top 5cm (2in) of soil and water when dry. Established in-ground plants are drought-tolerant and need deep watering roughly weekly with several gallons, especially 20 days before and after flowering.

recommendedMinLiters (19 L, a 5-gallon bucket) targets dwarf/container cultivars of max ~1m (3ft). Tall and giant cultivars (2-5m+) are really in-ground plants; an undersized container restricts roots, stunts growth and makes them top-heavy and prone to lodging (falling over).

Tall and giant cultivars (over ~2.4m / 8ft) typically need staking or a sturdy cane against wind and heavy seed heads; dwarf and semi-dwarf cultivars usually do not. Plant in a spot sheltered from strong winds.
Long taproot prefers a deep, loosened bed (dig/till ~60cm deep). Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizer, which encourages foliage over flowers and can weaken stems.
Generally low-maintenance; watch for slugs/snails on young plants, birds and squirrels on ripening seed heads, and occasional powdery/downy mildew or Sclerotinia rot in damp, crowded conditions.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Let's Grow Sunflowers: locations receiving 6 to 8 hours of direct sunlight are preferable; well-draining soil with pH 6-7.5; sow once soils warm above 50F (10C) and frost danger passes; heavy feeders benefit from regular slow-release fertilizer; large plants may need support for wind and top-heaviness from seed production.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC - Let's Grow Sunflowers: seeds planted once soils warm above 50F (10C) and frost danger passes; frost-sensitive warm-season annual.

RHS Growing Guide - Helianthus (sunflowers): full sun; annual sunflowers are thirsty plants needing consistently moist soil and mustn't dry out; container plants need watering whenever the compost feels dry, potentially daily in hot weather; feed annuals with potassium-rich tomato fertiliser to encourage flowering.

RHS Growing Guide - Helianthus (sunflowers): potassium-rich tomato fertiliser encourages flowering for annual types; sow indoors from late March, direct outdoors mid-April through May; transplant only once soil is warming and there is no frost risk after hardening off.

RHS Growing Guide - Helianthus (sunflowers): annual sunflowers cannot tolerate frost; most annuals need sturdy bamboo cane support and giant cultivars over 2.4m (8ft) need extra securing (e.g. against a wall); dwarf types are ~30cm (1ft) tall.

University of Minnesota Extension - Sunflowers: enjoy full sun; do best in slightly acidic soil pH 6.0-6.8, well-drained; direct seed after all danger of frost passes; can withstand some drought but water regularly 20 days before and after flowering; side dressing of all-purpose slow-release fertilizer when plants have several true leaves; giant (8ft+) cultivars sometimes require staking.
University of Minnesota Extension - Sunflowers: full sun; seeds germinate in 7-10 days; spacing 6in (2-5ft varieties) up to 2ft (giant cultivars); soil test recommended before planting.

Oregon State University Extension EM 9866 'Grow your own sunflowers': for containers choose dwarf cultivars max 3ft tall (less likely to become top-heavy); use a large container - a 5-gallon bucket (~19 L) with drainage holes or similar provides enough root space; well-draining potting mix; irrigate containers more frequently than in-ground; full sun 6-8+ hours; well-drained soil pH 6.0-7.5.

Oregon State University Extension EM 9866 'Grow your own sunflowers': use a balanced fertilizer (e.g. NPK 10-10-10) and avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers that promote leaf growth over flower formation; slow-release or split quick-release (half at planting, half at two sets of leaves); direct sow April-June after last frost when soil reaches at least 50F (10C); high winds can topple tall cultivars and staking helps.

Old Farmer's Almanac - Sunflowers: sow directly after danger of spring frost passes, once soils warm to at least 50F (10C); northern US/Canada April to mid-June, South mid-March or early April; full sun 6-8 hours; thrive in slightly acidic to alkaline soil pH 6.0-7.5; once established water deeply but infrequently, about once a week with several gallons; tall species and cultivars require support (bamboo stakes); feed sparingly to avoid weak stems.

Confidence note: wateringProfile.baseIntervalDays (3) is a conservative container-oriented estimate. Sources give qualitative watering guidance (RHS: water containers when compost dry, potentially daily in heat; UMN/Almanac: established in-ground deep watering ~weekly with several gallons) rather than a single fixed day interval; a 3-day base with a 2x hot-weather multiplier brackets both container and in-ground regimes. heatStressC (35C), maxSunHours (14) and rainSkipThresholdMm (10) are reasonable horticultural/care-engine estimates, not directly cited figures.

Confidence note: container volumes (recommendedMinLiters 19 = a 5-gallon bucket per OSU Extension, ideal 19-38 L) target dwarf/container cultivars (max ~3ft). Tall and giant cultivars are effectively in-ground plants and any single container minimum is an approximation; idealMaxLiters (38 L) is an estimate scaled for larger semi-dwarf types (see commonIssues).
